title: The Strait of Hormuz Already Faces a Tough Recovery. Now Trump's Iran Deal Is Unraveling. | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Five CFR experts argue Trump's 60-day Hormuz MOU is best understood as a bribe to Iran (up to $40B/yr) for safe passage rather than a nuclear deal, is already unraveling amid fresh strikes, and that the physical, military, infrastructure, and humanitarian obstacles mean the strait's reopening will not restore global energy markets or Gulf trade to the status quo ante.

themes.theme: The MOU as a Hormuz deal, not a nuclear deal

insights.claim: Trump's war aims shifted from regime change to denuclearization to demilitarization, but the June 17 MOU is best understood as a Hormuz deal: the US granted Iran sanctions relief purely in exchange for safe passage of commercial vessels, with no verifiable limits on Iran's nuclear program.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Reframes the deal's value: it is a fragile commercial arrangement over shipping, not a lasting security settlement—so it can collapse on shipping incidents alone.
insights.quote: The MOU is thus best understood less as a deal that addresses Iran's nuclear ambitions or military capabilities than as a Hormuz deal
insights.locator: Iran Won the Strait of Hormuz.

insights.claim: Roughly 20 percent of the world's oil and LNG transits the Strait of Hormuz, which is only twenty-one miles wide at its narrowest point.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Sizes the chokepoint exposure that keeps a persistent geopolitical risk premium in global energy prices.
insights.locator: The collapse of the sixty-day MOU

insights.claim: The fragile US-Iran cease-fire was in danger as both sides exchanged fresh strikes, the White House reimposed sanctions on Iranian oil, Iran's IRGC fired at US sites in Bahrain and Kuwait, and Trump declared of the MOU, 'To me, I think it's over.'
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: The deal underpinning any energy-market recovery is actively unraveling—near-term tail risk of re-escalation and renewed closure is live.
insights.quote: To me, I think it's over
insights.locator: The fragile U.S.-Iran cease-fire appeared to be in danger

themes.theme: Iran's control and monetization of the strait

insights.claim: Iran has no intention of restoring Hormuz as a free waterway: the MOU allows passage with no charge for 60 days only, and Tehran has seized on that language, working with Oman to define future 'administration and maritime services' and already striking vessels that transit without complying with Iranian protocols.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: A permanent Iranian regulatory/toll role in Hormuz is the base case—structurally raising transit costs and risk beyond the 60-day window.
insights.locator: Yet Iran has no intention of restoring

insights.claim: Iranian officials believe monetizing the strait could generate up to $40 billion a year—roughly equal to the country's recent annual oil export revenues—giving Tehran overwhelming incentives to retain control.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Quantifies why Iran will not relinquish control—a $40B/yr revenue prize makes toll enforcement, and recurring flashpoints, highly likely.
insights.locator: Controlling Hormuz offers Iran a deterrent

insights.claim: Fishman argues the best US strategy is patience: oil prices have fallen to near prewar levels, and as commercial stockpiles are replenished Iran's leverage diminishes—if Tehran overplays its hand it will only accelerate bypass pipelines, storage, and alternative energy that devalue the very prize it won.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Implies Iran's chokepoint leverage is self-limiting and time-decaying—watch stockpile replenishment and bypass investment as the leverage clock.
insights.quote: if it overplays its hand, it could devalue the very prize it has won
insights.locator: Iran may emerge from this war in control

themes.theme: Why there was no good military option

insights.claim: Trump signed a one-sided MOU because there was no easy, low-cost military option to reopen Hormuz: escorting merchant ships through a 21-mile-wide littoral negates the US Navy's blue-water advantages, and the Navy's mine-sweeping capabilities have atrophied while Iran laid fresh mines.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: The military-options constraint explains why the US paid a 'bribe'—and why future closures may again force concessions rather than force.
insights.locator: The Strait Remains a Military Minefield

insights.claim: Retired Admiral James Stavridis estimated that opening the strait militarily is a full-time job requiring an aircraft carrier with 80 aircraft, a dozen cruisers/destroyers, a half-dozen minesweepers, 75-plus USAF aircraft, 35 army helicopters, and 5,000-10,000 ground troops around the Iranian littoral.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Quantifies the prohibitive force package—reinforces that a durable military solution is politically and materially unlikely.
insights.quote: Opening the Strait militarily is a full-time job for an aircraft carrier with 80 Navy aircraft, a dozen guided missile cruisers/destroyers, a half-dozen minesweepers
insights.locator: The navy could probably accomplish the mission

insights.claim: Even a maximal operation would strain a US Navy with fewer than three hundred deployable warships and might still fail, because a single Iranian drone striking a single merchant ship would be enough to scare many shipowners away from the strait.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Highlights the asymmetry: cheap Iranian drones can deter expensive shipping indefinitely—keeping insurance and freight premia elevated.

themes.theme: Physical recovery, damage, and Gulf infrastructure

insights.claim: The disruption—a shut-in of more than 10 million barrels per day of oil and roughly 300 million cubic meters per day of LNG for over 100 days—has no precedent for unwinding, and damage to QatarEnergy's Ras Laffan LNG facilities is expected to take up to five years to repair.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Multi-year LNG damage means the supply hit outlasts the diplomatic timeline—structurally supportive for gas markets.
insights.locator: Why Hormuz's Reopening Won't Fix Global Energy Markets

insights.claim: An estimated eighty mines remain in the strait's main navigation areas, pushing seaborne exports onto alternate routes closer to Iran and Oman, while Chinese buyers snap up returning oil and Indian buyers hedge.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Routing dependence on Iran/Oman-adjacent lanes plus divergent China/India buying behavior are the swing variables for recovery pace.
insights.locator: Even if the strait's gradual reopening continues

insights.claim: There is $58 billion in damage to Gulf oil facilities, and Gulf states will deploy nearly 40 percent of the world's sovereign wealth to fund recovery and hardening—but existing pipelines cannot move the petroleum products vital to the global economy.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Massive sovereign-wealth-funded reconstruction and hardening is a durable capex theme, but a products-pipeline gap remains a bottleneck.
insights.locator: The Gulf's Recovery Depends on Cooperation

insights.claim: Recovery hinges on Gulf cooperation the region has long resisted: Saudi Arabia would expand its east-west Petroline to the Red Sea (potentially via Iraq, Turkey, even Syria), the UAE would expand its Abu Dhabi Crude Oil Pipeline and Fujairah and tap the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor, and Iraq/Kuwait may revive the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Maps the specific bypass-infrastructure buildout to watch—overland pipeline and corridor projects that reroute Gulf energy away from Hormuz.
insights.locator: The Gulf's Recovery Depends on Cooperation

insights.claim: It will take at least a year, likely longer, for Qatar to repair its Ras Laffan LNG complex, resulting in a tight market for 5 percent of the world's natural gas and 20 percent of its LNG supply.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Pins a concrete tightness figure on global gas/LNG—a sustained supportive factor for LNG prices through 2026-2027.

insights.claim: Forty-five million additional people are now facing acute food insecurity because of the war's cascading effect on food, fuel, and fertilizer markets, with Sudan, South Sudan, and Somalia at risk of famine in the coming months.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Quantifies the second-order humanitarian shock from Gulf energy disruption—fertilizer and food supply chains are the transmission channel.
insights.locator: Humanitarian Needs Will Have to Be Prioritized

insights.claim: The IMO is trying to evacuate more than 11,000 seafarers stranded in the Persian Gulf via a temporary maritime corridor, but Iran attacked a Singapore-flagged ship on an IMO route on June 25 and struck three more ships on July 7, exposing the limits of guaranteed safe passage.
insights.speaker: Edward Fishman
insights.so_what: Even UN-brokered humanitarian corridors are being struck—evidence that shipping risk stays high regardless of formal arrangements.
insights.locator: The IMO has a separate role to play
